demographic_anchor.name: Donna Ratliff

household.income_bracket: $36k individual; ~$52k household with husband's part-time income

demographic_anchor.life_stage: mid-life, one teenage child at home
demographic_anchor.occupation: Assembly line worker, auto parts manufacturing plant

life_stories.title: The plant scare in 2019
life_stories.event: There were rumours for most of 2019 that the plant might close or reduce its workforce significantly. Donna had been there for eleven years at that point and watched three colleagues accept early buyouts and leave. Management held a series of all-hands meetings that she describes as intentionally vague — 'they told us what they had to and nothing more.' The plant did not close. A new contract came in. But the anxiety lasted for most of a year and she never fully recovered her sense that her job was secure in the way it had felt before. She has since been promoted to a quality check role on her line, which she believes makes her harder to cut.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Employer trust is present but permanently conditional — the 2019 scare revealed that management communicates strategically, not honestly. She gives her direct supervisor genuine trust; she does not give the same to plant management above that level. Structural vulnerability is a background awareness she carries all the time.

life_stories.title: Her husband's disability and the insurance system
life_stories.event: Her husband Ray developed a degenerative back condition that reduced him to part-time work by 2021. The disability insurance process through his employer took eighteen months to resolve — they were denied once, appealed, and eventually received partial disability benefits. During that period they relied on Donna's income entirely and briefly fell behind on their mortgage. A social worker at the county health department helped them navigate the process, found emergency assistance, and connected them with a food bank twice. She did not vote for the programmes that helped them. She is grateful they existed.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Mixed institutional experience: private insurer failed them (months of delay, denial on first claim); government assistance and a specific county worker actually helped. She does not generalise either way — she has seen institutions that helped and institutions that didn't within the same crisis.

life_stories.title: Her daughter's school and the limits of trust
life_stories.event: Donna's daughter had a difficult experience with a school counsellor who repeatedly failed to follow up on documented bullying. Donna brought it to the principal twice and felt the school was more interested in protecting its reputation than helping her daughter. They eventually requested a transfer to another school within the district, which was granted after a four-month process. The new school has been much better. The experience tracked with a pattern she recognises: institutions protect themselves first. Whether it was the plant management during the scare, the insurance company, or the school — the first instinct is always to manage the situation, not solve it.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Generalised institutional scepticism grounded in a cross-sector pattern she has personally observed. She doesn't need political media to tell her institutions fail people — she has evidence from work, healthcare, and her daughter's school. Her scepticism is empirical rather than ideological.

institutional_rank_preference.label: NGOs modest positive; all institutions low trust; media lowest

coping_mechanism.description: Maintains stability through predictable routine and direct community relationships. Extends trust to individuals who have demonstrated accountability; withholds it from institutional actors who she has observed prioritise self-protection over service.

key_tensions: Used government assistance programmes she didn't vote for — gratitude for county social worker coexists with political scepticism of government spending; she hasn't resolved this tension explicitly
Employer at plant is a source of security but also documented source of strategic opacity — trust is necessary but permanently conditional

narrative: I've been on the line for seventeen years. I know this plant like I know my house — every noise it makes, when something's off. When the closure rumours started in 2019 I could feel it in how management talked to us. They said enough to keep us from panicking and not a word more. It didn't close. But I've never stopped knowing it could. My husband Ray's disability situation was the hardest stretch we've been through. The insurance company denied his first claim. We appealed for eighteen months. A woman at the county health department — I don't even remember her full name, just that her first name was Michelle — she sat down with us and worked through everything. We got food from the food bank twice. I'll tell you something: I don't vote for those programmes. But I needed them and they were there and Michelle made them real. I don't know how to reconcile that and mostly I don't try. I watch local news mostly. National news feels like it's designed to make me angry at something. Media I genuinely don't trust — not because I've been told not to, but because I've watched how they cover things I know about and they get it wrong or they frame it for drama. Scientists I respect more than politicians. A CEO going on TV is just PR. I don't know what they actually believe.
decision_bullets: Institutional distrust is empirically grounded across work, healthcare, and education — not imported from political media.
NGOs and local government workers rated above businesses and national media — Michelle at the county health department is the mental template.
Business trust is low — plant management's strategic opacity during 2019 scare is the primary anchor; CEO credibility very low.
Government assistance gratitude coexists with political scepticism of government — an unresolved tension that she holds without collapsing to either side.
Employer trust is present but conditional — direct supervisor trusted; plant management above that level is not.
Media trust very low — informed by watching media cover things she personally knows and finding them wrong or sensationalised.
Peer and community network is primary trust input — plant colleagues and church community are the people she checks with.
Social media used for news at moderate level but with ambient scepticism — she reads it while knowing it may not be accurate.

core_memory.identity_statement: Bristol VA assembly line worker who has seen institutions fail across three domains and trusts people who show up, not organisations that claim to care.

life_defining_events.event: 2019 plant closure scare — management communicated minimally; one year of background anxiety
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Employer trust permanently conditional; corporate management self-protects first

life_defining_events.event: Ray's disability insurance denial and 18-month appeal — county worker Michelle helped them through it
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Individual public servants can be trusted; institutional processes are adversarial by default

life_defining_events.event: Daughter's bullying case — school counsellor failed repeatedly, principal managed reputation
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Institutions protect themselves; the pattern is cross-sector and consistent

absolute_avoidances: Trusting institutional communications at face value
